title: Point of Order! How Missouri Baptists Killed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.
author: Adam Cochrun
orientation: Adam Cochrun recounts how, at the 2021 Missouri Baptist Convention annual meeting, his motion to bring a Resolution on Abolishing Abortion to the floor was nearly tabled by procedural oversight and was ultimately defeated by Pastor/State Representative Doug Richey's argumentation. The piece blends parliamentary critique with a theological rebuke of incrementalist pro-life politics among Southern Baptists.
audiences: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
Christian state legislators and civil magistrates

questions.question: What procedural failure nearly killed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at the 2021 Missouri Baptist annual meeting?
questions.answer: The resolutions committee was supposed to bring Cochrun's motion (and one other) to the floor at the close of their report but instead walked off stage after presenting only their own five resolutions. Cochrun's call of 'point of order' forced the committee to return so the convention could actually consider the motion.
questions.quote: The resolutions committee was supposed to present my motion (as well as one other motion) at the end of their resolutions report, but instead, left the stage after presenting their own five resolutions. Had I not called for a point of order, they would have killed two valid motions by simply walking away from them.

questions.question: How does Cochrun frame the duty of Spirit-indwelt Christians toward the legal status of abortion?
questions.answer: He argues that because Ephesians 3 teaches the Holy Spirit dwells within believers, those Christians cannot settle for celebrating gradual regulation of 'child sacrifice' but must call for full justice, in keeping with Eph. 4:1's command to 'walk in a manner worthy of the calling.'
questions.quote: Eph. 4:1 is indeed an appropriate admonition because Ephesians 3 teaches us that the Holy Spirit dwells within us! Must those indwelt with God's very Spirit call for justice in regards to child sacrifice, or should they celebrate the gradual regulation of child sacrifice?

questions.question: Why does Cochrun believe it is sometimes appropriate to publicly name fellow believers in disputes like this?
questions.answer: He disagrees with President Jon Nelson's caution against 'name dropping,' arguing that when a public figure's behavior is both true and sinful enough to warrant rebuke, naming them is appropriate, citing Senator Bob Onder gutting Mike Moon's abolition bill as a case in point.
questions.quote: It is sometimes appropriate and necessary to name names if the behavior of the public figure being brought to light is both true and sinful to the point of requiring public rebuke. Senator Bob Onder gutting Senator Mike Moon's bill to abolish abortion undoubtedly falls into this category.

questions.question: What did Doug Richey concede about Roe v. Wade and how did Cochrun characterize that admission?
questions.audience: Christian state legislators and civil magistrates
questions.answer: Richey correctly affirmed that Roe is not the law of the land but said he treats it as if it were 'because others do.' Cochrun reads this as evidence of following the crowd into evil rather than standing on unpopular truth before the Missouri General Assembly.
questions.quote: First, you correctly affirmed that Roe v. Wade is not the law of the land. Second, you stated that you treat it as if it is "because others do." These first two answers demonstrate that you are following the multitude to do evil (Exodus 23:2) rather than standing firmly on truth that is unpopular among the Missouri General Assembly.

questions.question: What is Cochrun's central objection to incremental pro-life legislation?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
questions.answer: He argues that drawing arbitrary legal lines about gestational age, location, or method effectively writes injustice into law by instructing abortionists and parents on the permissible conditions for killing preborn children, regardless of any reduction in abortion numbers.
questions.quote: It is undeniable that incremental pro-life legislation instructs abortionists and abortion-minded parents when, where, why, and how they may murder preborn children. You may argue that telling Missourians when, where, why, and how to murder children is a good thing if it reduces abortion numbers, but you cannot argue that you are not writing injustice into law.

questions.question: How does Cochrun diagnose the deeper intellectual and spiritual problem behind politicians like Richey?
questions.answer: He attributes their reasoning to secularist, pragmatic, man-centered thinking that has been seeping into mainstream Christianity since at least the Enlightenment, leaving such politicians blind to the repentance the situation requires.
questions.quote: Sadly, this is what secularism has done to Doug's thinking, but Doug is not alone. This sort of pragmatic, man-centered thinking has been leaking into mainstream Christanity since at least the enlightenment.

questions.question: What 'red herring' argument does Cochrun say defeated the resolution at both the 2020 and 2021 meetings?
questions.answer: He says messengers were misled by Richey's claim that the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion would prevent him from signing incrementalist bills, distracting them from the better question of why incrementalist bills are needed at all when legislators could simply unite to abolish abortion outright.
questions.quote: Returning to Richey's testimony against the resolution, the argument that deluded the messengers of both the 2020 and 2021 annual meetings is the idea that the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion will restrict him from signing incrementalist bills. This is a red herring, or whatever shiny thing Doug thinks would distract his audience who relies on his expertise from asking the crucial question: "Why do you need to support incrementalist bills when you can simply stand with other abolitionists in the Missouri House and Senate to abolish abortion?"

questions.question: Why did abolitionists want to strike the call to end taxpayer-funded abortion from Resolution 5?
questions.answer: Cochrun argues such a demand is too weak for the evil at hand: an abolitionist document should demand the total abolition of preborn murder without exception or compromise, not merely its defunding.
questions.quote: This is why I and other abolitionists wanted to strike from Resolution 5, had time permitted, the irrelevant call for the end of taxpayer funded abortion, which does not belong in a truly abolitionist document. Some demands are too weak for a given evil, and we must not demand the mere defunding, but the total abolition, of the murder of our preborn neighbors without exception or compromise.

questions.question: What does Cochrun say is actually preventing the Missouri state government from establishing justice for all preborn children?
questions.audience: Christian state legislators and civil magistrates
questions.answer: He claims it is not lack of support but the preoccupation of pro-life politicians with producing a steady stream of incrementalist 'save some' bills instead of standing with abolitionists in the legislature to abolish abortion outright.
questions.quote: The only thing keeping the state government from establishing justice for all preborn children is the fact that the pro-life politicians are consumed with producing a never-ending stream of incrementalist bills that "save some."

questions.question: What ongoing posture does Cochrun call Christians to take toward civil magistrates?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
questions.answer: He calls Christians to keep crying 'point of order,' continually reproving, rebuking, and exhorting magistrates—especially those claiming Christ as Lord—and praying that God would grant them repentance.
questions.quote: Therefore, we must continue to reprove, rebuke, and exhort them (2 Tim. 4:2);  praying for them (1 Tim 2:2) to walk worthy of the calling to which they were called. May God grant us repentance (2 Tim. 2:25).

related.title: Hearing for Abolition of Abortion in Missouri Act Scheduled for Wednesday 3-10
related.reason: Provides background on the Missouri abolition bill effort that Sen. Mike Moon championed and that Sen. Bob Onder is criticized in this article for gutting.

related.title: Missouri is Not Abortion-Free, But Thanks to Sen. Mike Moon, It Can Be
related.reason: Explains why Missouri's existing pro-life laws fall short and ties directly to the legislative dynamics behind the Missouri Baptist resolution debate.

related.title: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
related.reason: Profiles the Southern Baptist legislator whose abolition bill is referenced as having been gutted, contextualizing the political stakes Cochrun describes.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: Counterpoint at the national SBC level: shows what success looks like when a Baptist body actually passes an abolition resolution, the very outcome denied in Missouri.

related.title: Arkansas Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at State Convention
related.reason: Parallel state-convention case where the resolution passed, illuminating by contrast what Missouri Baptists rejected.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Develops in detail the principled case against incrementalism that Cochrun summarizes when rebutting Doug Richey's reasoning.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism In a Nutshell
related.reason: Primer on the core abolitionist/pro-life dispute that frames the entire Missouri Baptist controversy described in this article.
